refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint derived from human factors and safety analysis. Refinement clarifies that latency is end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both directions of communication, and is measured under realistic network conditions agreed with stakeholders.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor acquisition through network transmission to operator display and back through command transmission to actuator execution. Conduct latency profiling under nominal and degraded network conditions (packet loss, jitter, congestion). Validate p95 percentile across representative operational scenarios with Site Manager and Tele-operator.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire and encode sensor data (video, telemetry) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor data over network link with bounded latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive and decode sensor data at operator station with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Receive operator commands at operator station with minimal latency
FR-TEL-005: Transmit operator commands over network link with bounded latency
FR-TEL-006: Receive and execute commands on vehicle with minimal latency
FR-TEL-007: Monitor and adapt communication to maintain latency bounds under network degradation

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement end-to-end latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator display, and command execution; use hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding and priority-queued network transmission
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (private 5G or dedicated fiber) with QoS guarantees and redundant paths; minimize hops and processing delays
DP-C: Implement adaptive codec selection and frame-rate adjustment based on network conditions; use predictive control algorithms to mask latency
DP-D: Combine latency budget allocation (DP-A) with adaptive network optimization (DP-C) and QoS-enabled transport (DP-B); add telemetry monitoring and closed-loop latency control
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer latency management strategy combining: (1) explicit end-to-end latency budget allocation across all subsystems (sensor→network→operator→command→vehicle), (2) hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding to minimize processing delays, (3) QoS-enabled network transport with priority queuing, (4) adaptive codec and frame-rate selection responding to network conditions, (5) real-time latency telemetry collection and closed-loop control to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms, and (6) predictive/compensatory control algorithms to mask residual latency effects.

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition latency (FR-TEL-001) and network transmission (FR-TEL-002): both must be optimized jointly; cannot reduce network latency if sensor encoding is slow.
Coupling between network QoS (DP-B element) and operator display rendering (FR-TEL-003): display refresh rate and decoding must align with network delivery rate.
Coupling between adaptive codec selection (DP-C) and command execution (FR-TEL-006): codec choice affects bandwidth and latency; must not exceed vehicle actuator response time.
Coupling between latency monitoring (FR-TEL-007) and all other sub-FRs: telemetry collection itself adds latency; must be designed to minimize overhead.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a coupled design that requires careful orchestration across multiple subsystems. The latency budget allocation (DP-A component) provides a framework to manage these couplings. Adaptive mechanisms (DP-C component) introduce feedback loops that must be stable and responsive. Recommend detailed latency budget spreadsheet and simulation before implementation.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure (agreed conditions) provides baseline latency < 80 ms; remaining budget (70 ms) allocated to sensor, operator, and vehicle subsystems.
ASSUMPTION: Hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding (GPU or ASIC) is available on both vehicle and operator station.
ASSUMPTION: Operator station and vehicle can support real-time telemetry feedback for latency monitoring without adding significant overhead.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed baseline, adaptive mechanisms may not be sufficient to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms; fallback to degraded-mode operation required.
RISK: Predictive/compensatory control algorithms may introduce instability if tuned incorrectly; requires extensive validation and operator training.
RISK: Latency monitoring telemetry itself consumes bandwidth and processing; must be carefully designed to avoid adding latency.
RISK: Codec adaptation may introduce visual artifacts or command delays if switching is too frequent; requires hysteresis and operator feedback.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) latency budget allocation table (sensor, network, operator, vehicle subsystems with target latencies), (2) hardware acceleration requirements (GPU/ASIC specs), (3) QoS parameters (priority levels, packet loss tolerance), (4) adaptive algorithm thresholds (when to switch codecs, frame rates), (5) telemetry overhead limits (max % of bandwidth for monitoring), and (6) fallback/degradation modes if latency bounds cannot be maintained. Recommend creating a detailed latency model and simulation before proceeding to IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency management strategy, including sensor data acquisition and encoding, network transmission optimization, operator display rendering and decoding, command reception and transmission, and real-time latency monitoring and adaptation. This module integrates hardware-accelerated codecs, QoS-enabled network transport, adaptive algorithms, and telemetry collection. It interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor input and command output), Safety Services (for latency-related safety alerts), and external Network Link (for transport).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs sensor data (video, IMU, GPS, load cells, etc.) encoded and packetized for transmission. Telemetry includes raw sensor streams and processed telemetry for operator display. Latency of telemetry output is a critical design parameter.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from the operator station. Tasking input latency and command execution timing are critical for safe teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional communication. Network Link provides transport with QoS parameters (latency, jitter, packet loss). Teleoperation module monitors and adapts to Network Link conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs SafetyAlert signals if latency exceeds safe thresholds or network conditions degrade beyond acceptable limits. Safety Services consumes these alerts to trigger operator warnings or emergency stop.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation latency management is a critical subsystem function but not the entire NGHE solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and make latency ownership unclear. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and command execution on the vehicle side. However, teleoperation latency is end-to-end and includes operator station, network, and vehicle components. Assigning to Vehicle alone would miss operator-side and network-side latency management. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is explicitly designed to own remote operation capabilities, including communication, latency management, and operator-vehicle interaction. This is the natural and intended home for end-to-end latency management. High containment, minimal diffusion. RECOMMENDED.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerts. Latency management is a safety-critical function, but Safety Services should consume latency alerts from Teleoperation, not own the latency management itself. Assigning here would create circular dependencies and diffuse latency ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the appropriate and natural owner of end-to-end latency management for CR-NGHE-008. Teleoperation is explicitly scoped to manage remote operation, including communication, encoding/decoding, network optimization, and operator-vehicle interaction. Latency is a core performance attribute of teleoperation. Containment score of 0.95 reflects strong alignment. Diffusion score of 0.05 reflects minimal risk of responsibility fragmentation.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may not own the Vehicle subsystem's sensor acquisition hardware or command execution actuators. Latency budget allocation requires coordination with Vehicle module for sensor-side and actuator-side latency targets. Recommend explicit interface contract and latency SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle.

RISK: Network Link is external to Teleoperation. Teleoperation must adapt to Network Link conditions but cannot directly control network infrastructure. Recommend clear interface specification for Network Link QoS parameters and monitoring.

RISK: Operator station hardware (display, input devices) may be outside Teleoperation module scope if operator station is a separate system. Latency management requires coordination with operator station design. Recommend explicit interface contract for operator-side latency targets.
RISK: Safety Services must be notified of latency violations. Teleoperation must output SafetyAlert signals reliably. Recommend clear alert thresholds and escalation procedures.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module scope to explicitly include: (1) sensor data acquisition and encoding on vehicle side (or interface contract with Vehicle for latency targets), (2) operator display rendering and decoding on operator station side (or interface contract with operator station for latency targets), (3) network transport optimization and QoS management (or interface contract with Network Link for QoS parameters), (4) real-time latency monitoring and telemetry collection, (5) adaptive codec and frame-rate selection, (6) SafetyAlert generation for latency violations. Create detailed interface specifications and latency SLAs with Vehicle, operator station, and Network Link. Recommend latency budget allocation table as part of Teleoperation design documentation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layer latency management strategy) directly addresses CR-NGHE-008 (teleoperation latency ≤ 150 ms p95). DP-D includes latency budget allocation, hardware acceleration, QoS-enabled transport, adaptive algorithms, and monitoring—all necessary to achieve the acceptance criterion. DP-D is not over-constrained (allows multiple implementation paths) and not under-constrained (provides sufficient guidance for design). Coupling analysis identifies potential interactions but DP-D provides a framework (latency budget) to manage them. FR-DP mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle module for sensor acquisition and command execution latency. Recommend explicit latency SLA and interface contract.
Teleoperation module must coordinate with operator station (may be external system) for display rendering latency. Recommend explicit interface specification.
Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for QoS guarantees. Network Link is external; Teleoperation must adapt to network conditions. Recommend clear QoS parameter interface.
Safety Services must be notified of latency violations. Teleoperation must reliably output SafetyAlert signals. Recommend clear alert thresholds and escalation procedures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) for end-to-end latency management. Diffusion risks are primarily at module boundaries (Vehicle, operator station, Network Link, Safety Services). These are manageable via explicit interface contracts and SLAs. Teleoperation is the appropriate owner of latency management logic and coordination.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be instrumented with latency telemetry to measure sensor-to-operator-to-actuator latency. Verification requires test scenarios with Site Manager, Equipment Operator, and Tele-operator. Acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Teleoperation module is clearly allocated responsibility for end-to-end latency management. Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-007) decompose latency management into sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator display, command reception, command transmission, vehicle execution, and monitoring. Each sub-FR can be allocated to specific Teleoperation subsystems or interfaces. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Create detailed latency budget allocation table (sensor, network, operator, vehicle subsystems) with target latencies for each component. Ensure sum of component latencies ≤ 150 ms p95.
Define explicit interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle, operator station, Network Link, and Safety Services. Include latency SLAs and QoS parameters.
Specify hardware acceleration requirements (GPU/ASIC) for encoding/decoding to ensure sensor and operator-side latency targets are achievable.
Define adaptive algorithm thresholds (codec switching, frame-rate adjustment) and hysteresis to avoid oscillation.
Specify telemetry overhead limits (max % of bandwidth for latency monitoring) to ensure monitoring does not add significant latency.
Define fallback/degradation modes if latency bounds cannot be maintained (e.g., reduced frame rate, lower resolution, operator warning).
Conduct latency simulation and modeling before detailed design to validate DP-D feasibility and identify critical path items.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is a complex, coupled requirement that spans multiple subsystems (sensor acquisition, network transport, operator display, command execution, monitoring). The refined FR and selected DP-D provide a sound framework for latency management, but the requirement is not atomic and requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-007) have been identified and should be decomposed further to allocate latency budgets, specify hardware acceleration, define adaptive algorithms, and establish interface contracts. Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner, but detailed design requires decomposition of sub-FRs to subsystem level. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire and encode sensor data (video, telemetry) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor data over network link with bounded latency and jitter
FR-TEL-003: Receive and decode sensor data at operator station with minimal latency
FR-TEL-004: Receive operator commands at operator station with minimal latency
FR-TEL-005: Transmit operator commands over network link with bounded latency
FR-TEL-006: Receive and execute commands on vehicle with minimal latency
FR-TEL-007: Monitor and adapt communication to maintain latency bounds under network degradation

assumptions: Network infrastructure (agreed conditions) provides baseline latency < 80 ms; remaining budget (70 ms) allocated to sensor, operator, and vehicle subsystems.
Hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding (GPU or ASIC) is available on both vehicle and operator station.
Operator station and vehicle can support real-time telemetry feedback for latency monitoring without adding significant overhead.
Safety Services will consume SafetyAlert signals from Teleoperation and trigger appropriate operator warnings or emergency stop.
Vehicle module will provide sensor data with known latency characteristics and accept commands with known execution latency.
Network Link will provide QoS parameters (latency, jitter, packet loss) and support priority queuing for teleoperation traffic.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed baseline, making p95 ≤ 150 ms unachievable. Fallback/degradation modes required.
Predictive/compensatory control algorithms may introduce instability if tuned incorrectly. Extensive validation and operator training required.
Latency monitoring telemetry may add significant overhead if not carefully designed. Recommend overhead limits and optimization.
Codec adaptation may introduce visual artifacts or command delays if switching is too frequent. Hysteresis and operator feedback required.
Operator station hardware (display, input devices) may not meet latency targets. Coordination with operator station design required.
Vehicle sensor acquisition and command execution may not meet latency targets. Coordination with Vehicle module required.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation table with target latencies for each subsystem (sensor, network, operator, vehicle).
Hardware acceleration specifications (GPU/ASIC) for encoding/decoding with measured latency performance.
Network QoS parameters (latency, jitter, packet loss) under agreed network conditions.
Adaptive algorithm specifications (codec switching, frame-rate adjustment) with stability analysis.
Telemetry overhead analysis (bandwidth, processing) to ensure monitoring does not add significant latency.
Fallback/degradation mode specifications for network degradation scenarios.
Latency simulation and modeling results validating DP-D feasibility.
Interface contracts and SLAs between Teleoperation and Vehicle, operator station, Network Link, Safety Services.
Operator training and validation results for predictive/compensatory control algorithms.
End-to-end latency measurement results from test scenarios with Site Manager, Equipment Operator, and Tele-operator.
